nodes.name: Spirits & Cocktails
nodes.description: Distilled spirits, cocktail origins, and key ingredients

items.prompt: Which spirit, flavored primarily with juniper berries, originated as a medicinal Dutch liquor called genever before evolving into the London Dry style that became the base of countless classic cocktails?
items.explanation:
Derived from genever (Dutch/Flemish for juniper), a medicinal liquor developed in the Netherlands in the 17th century
British soldiers drank genever during the Thirty Years' War — giving rise to the phrase 'Dutch courage'
Gin Act of 1751 in Britain curtailed a social crisis of cheap gin consumption among the urban poor (the 'Gin Craze')
London Dry gin: redistilled neutral spirit with botanicals including juniper, coriander, angelica root, citrus peel; 'dry' means no post-distillation sugar
Other styles: Plymouth, Old Tom (sweeter), Sloe Gin (infused with sloe berries), contemporary craft gins
The Martini, Negroni, Tom Collins, Gimlet, and Singapore Sling are all gin-based classics
Gin is required by EU law to have a predominant juniper flavor

items.prompt: Which American whiskey, distilled primarily in Kentucky, must by law contain at least 51% corn in the mash and be aged in new charred oak barrels, giving it its characteristic vanilla and caramel notes?
items.explanation:
Named after Bourbon County, Kentucky, though it can legally be made anywhere in the United States
Legal requirements: ≥51% corn mash, distilled to no more than 160 proof, barreled at no more than 125 proof, no additives other than water, aged in new charred American oak barrels
New charred oak barrels are used only once for Bourbon; spent barrels are often sold to Scotch producers
Kentucky accounts for ~95% of the world's Bourbon supply; climate swings in the state accelerate oak interaction
The 'Bourbon Belt': Maker's Mark, Jim Beam, Wild Turkey, Four Roses, Buffalo Trace all based in Kentucky
Defined as a 'distinctive product of the United States' by Congress in 1964
Straight Bourbon must be aged at least 2 years; 'bottled in bond' means at least 4 years, from one distillery, one season
items.cardBack: American whiskey (Kentucky, ≥51% corn, new charred oak)

items.prompt: Which whisky, produced in Scotland from malted barley (for single malt) or grain, aged at least 3 years in oak casks, and distinguished by regional styles from smoky Islay to floral Speyside?
items.explanation:
Must be produced in Scotland, aged at least 3 years in oak casks, and bottled at a minimum of 40% ABV
Five protected regions: Highlands, Lowlands, Speyside (most distilleries), Islay (peaty, smoky), Campbeltown
Single malt: from a single distillery, 100% malted barley; blended Scotch (e.g., Johnnie Walker, Chivas) mixes malts and grain whiskies
Note the spelling: Scotch is 'whisky' (no 'e'); American and Irish versions are 'whiskey'
Peat smoke (from burning peat to dry malted barley) gives Islay whiskies (Laphroaig, Ardbeg, Lagavulin) their medicinal, smoky character
Speyside whiskies (Glenfiddich, Macallan, Glenlivet) tend to be lighter, fruitier, and less peated
Single malt Scotch exports exceed £5 billion annually; the US is the largest market by value

items.prompt: Which French brandy, produced in the Charente region from Ugni Blanc grapes and double-distilled in copper pot stills before oak aging, is graded by age as VS, VSOP, or XO?
items.explanation:
AOC spirit from the Charente and Charente-Maritime departments of western France
Primary grape: Ugni Blanc (Trebbiano), chosen for its high acidity and low alcohol — ideal for distillation, not drinking fresh
Must be double-distilled in traditional Charentais copper pot stills (alembic)
Aged in French Limousin or Tronçais oak barrels; the portion that evaporates annually is called 'the angel's share'
Age grades: VS (Very Special, ≥2 years), VSOP (≥4 years), XO (≥10 years since 2018)
Six sub-appellations: Grande Champagne and Petite Champagne (best soils) are most prized; Grande Champagne has the longest aging potential
Major houses: Hennessy, Rémy Martin, Martell, Courvoisier — the 'Big Four' control about 90% of global Cognac sales

items.prompt: Which Mexican spirit, produced exclusively from blue agave plants in the state of Jalisco (and four other designated states), must contain at least 51% agave but bears a '100% agave' label only when no other sugars are added?
items.explanation:
Named after the city of Tequila in Jalisco, Mexico; Denomination of Origin protection since 1974
Made from blue Weber agave (Agave tequilana); the piña (heart of the plant) is harvested after 7–12 years of growth
Types by aging: Blanco/Plata (unaged), Reposado (2 months–1 year in oak), Añejo (1–3 years), Extra Añejo (3+ years)
'100% agave' on the label means no added sugars; mixto tequila can use up to 49% non-agave sugars
Mezcal is a broader category; all tequila is mezcal, but not all mezcal is tequila — mezcal uses various agave species
The worm ('gusano') is associated with mezcal, not tequila; its presence is a marketing gimmick, not tradition
Tequila's major brands: Jose Cuervo (world's best-selling), Patrón, Don Julio, Herradura

items.prompt: Which spirit, distilled from sugarcane juice or molasses and historically associated with the Caribbean and the colonial triangle trade, ranges in style from light Puerto Rican whites to heavy aged Jamaican darks?
items.explanation:
First distilled in the Caribbean in the 17th century, most likely in Barbados, from molasses — a byproduct of sugar refining
The Triangle Trade: British ships carried rum to West Africa, enslaved people to the Caribbean, and sugar/molasses back to Britain
Light rum (Puerto Rico, Cuba): column-distilled, neutral, used in Mojitos and Daiquiris; dark rum (Jamaica, Barbados): pot-distilled, heavier, more congeners
Rhum agricole (Martinique, Guadeloupe): distilled from fresh sugarcane juice rather than molasses — earthy, grassy, AOC-protected
The British Royal Navy issued daily rum rations to sailors from 1655 until 1970 ('Black Tot Day')
Spiced rum adds cinnamon, vanilla, and other spices after distillation; popular example: Captain Morgan
Aged rum from Barbados (Mount Gay), Jamaica (Appleton), and Trinidad (Angostura) are considered benchmark producers

items.prompt: Which neutral spirit, produced by multiple distillations and filtration to remove almost all flavor, has disputed origins between Russia and Poland, and is the world's best-selling spirit by volume?
items.explanation:
Name derives from Slavic 'voda' (water) — 'little water'; earliest records appear in Russia (9th century) and Poland (8th century documents cited by Polish side)
Russia and Poland have both legally claimed the right to call vodka their national spirit; the EU recognized both in 1992
Can be made from any fermentable material: grain (wheat, rye), potato, grapes, or sugarcane — base barely affects the final flavor
Multiple distillations and carbon filtration create a neutral spirit; EU requires minimum 37.5% ABV, US requires 40%
Grey Goose, Belvedere, Tito's, Smirnoff, and Absolut are major global brands
Vodka Belt countries (Russia, Poland, Belarus, Baltic states) account for the highest per-capita consumption
Became popular in the US in the 1950s after Smirnoff's 'It leaves you breathless' campaign

items.prompt: Which classic cocktail of gin and dry vermouth, garnished with an olive or a lemon twist, became culturally iconic when James Bond specified his preferred preparation as 'shaken, not stirred'?
items.explanation:
Combines gin and dry vermouth; proportions have shifted over time from near-equal parts to very gin-heavy ('dry' Martini)
The garnish signals intent: olive = savory, lemon twist = citrus-bright; a dirty Martini includes olive brine
James Bond's preference ('shaken not stirred') appears in Ian Fleming's novels from 1953 onward; shaking actually bruises and dilutes gin faster than stirring
The Vesper Martini (Casino Royale, 1953): gin + vodka + Kina Lillet; Bond's personal recipe
Precursor: the Martinez cocktail (1880s San Francisco or New York), made with sweet vermouth and Old Tom gin
FDR's Martini: reportedly 2:1 gin-to-vermouth with a dash of olive brine — shaken, of course
The 'Martini glass' (conical coupe) is widely associated with the cocktail though many bartenders now prefer a coupe or rocks glass

items.prompt: Which whiskey cocktail of rye or bourbon, sweet vermouth, and Angostura bitters, stirred and served in a coupe with a cherry, is most commonly associated with New York City's 1870s drinking culture?
items.explanation:
Combines rye or bourbon with sweet (Italian-style) vermouth and Angostura bitters; stirred, strained into a coupe, garnished with a Luxardo maraschino cherry
First documented in print in 1882 in a newspaper in Olean, New York: 'whiskey, vermouth, and bitters'
The popular origin story — created at the Manhattan Club in 1874 for a banquet hosted by Lady Randolph Churchill — has been largely debunked (Churchill was in England at the time)
Rye whiskey is the traditional base; Bourbon Manhattans are sweeter and more modern
A 'Perfect Manhattan' uses half sweet vermouth, half dry vermouth
The Rob Roy is a Scotch whisky Manhattan; the Toronto substitutes Fernet-Branca for bitters
The Manhattan is considered the first true 'modern' cocktail — it established the template of spirit + vermouth + bitters

items.prompt: Which cocktail — considered the oldest style of cocktail by definition — consists of a spirit (traditionally rye or bourbon), sugar, bitters, and water, with an orange peel and cherry as garnish?
items.explanation:
The 'original' cocktail format: spirit + sugar + bitters + water; definition published in 1806 in The Balance and Columbian Repository
The name 'Old Fashioned' emerged when drinkers in the 1880s–1890s asked for cocktails 'the old-fashioned way' before muddled fruit and soda water became popular additions
Traditional preparation: sugar cube muddled with Angostura bitters and a splash of water, then spirit poured over ice
Bourbon is the modern standard; Rye is more traditional; Wisconsin-style adds muddled fruit (orange, cherry) — controversial among purists
Popularized by Don Draper's on-screen preference in Mad Men (2007–2015), causing a surge in craft cocktail interest
The Sazerac (rye + Peychaud's bitters + absinthe rinse) from New Orleans is a close cousin and may be older
Pros debate whether to muddle the fruit or use only the peel as garnish
items.cardBack: Spirit + sugar + bitters + water (oldest cocktail format)

items.prompt: Which Italian aperitivo cocktail of equal parts gin, Campari, and sweet vermouth, garnished with an orange peel, was allegedly created in Florence around 1919 when Count Camillo Negroni asked for an Americano strengthened with gin?
items.explanation:
Equal parts gin, Campari, and sweet vermouth; stirred, served over ice, garnished with an orange peel
Most widely accepted origin: Caffè Casoni (now Caffè Giacosa), Florence, Italy, around 1919 — Count Camillo Negroni asked bartender Fosco Scarselli to replace the soda water in his Americano with gin
The Americano (Campari + sweet vermouth + soda) is the Negroni's predecessor; the Sbagliato replaces gin with Prosecco
Campari's distinctive red color originally came from carmine (cochineal insects); the formula changed in 2006
Negroni Sbagliato ('mistaken Negroni') went viral in 2022 after actress Emma D'Arcy (House of the Dragon) named it their drink of choice in an HBO interview
The Boulevardier substitutes Bourbon for gin — essentially a Whiskey Negroni
Negroni Week (annual charity event started by Imbibe magazine in 2013) raises millions for charitable causes

items.prompt: Which Cuban highball cocktail of white rum, fresh lime juice, sugar, mint, and soda water became a global summertime staple and was reportedly a favorite drink of Ernest Hemingway in Havana?
items.explanation:
Cuban origin; name may derive from 'mojito' (a diminutive of mojo, meaning 'little spell' or from African word 'mojo' used for magic)
Core ingredients: white rum, fresh lime juice, cane sugar (or simple syrup), fresh mint leaves, and sparkling soda water
Hemingway reportedly frequented La Bodeguita del Medio in Havana — legend says he inscribed 'My mojito in La Bodeguita' on the wall, though historians dispute this
The mojito predates Hemingway: a 16th-century predecessor called El Draque used aguardiente, lime, sugar, and mint
Muddling the mint: pressing lightly releases essential oils; over-muddling produces a bitter, broken-leaf drink
Cuba Libre (rum + cola + lime) is a simpler Cuban rum drink; the Daiquiri (rum + lime + sugar) is its shaken counterpart
The Mojito is among the five most popular cocktails globally by bar orders

items.prompt: Which Mexican cocktail of tequila, lime juice, and triple sec (orange liqueur), typically served with a salted rim, is the most ordered cocktail in the United States and has multiple disputed origin stories?
items.explanation:
Consists of tequila, fresh lime juice, and triple sec (Cointreau or Combier); shaken or blended, served in a glass with a salted rim
The salted rim balances the sweetness of the orange liqueur and the tartness of lime
Disputed origin: multiple bartenders in Mexico and Texas in the 1930s–1940s each claim to have invented it; no single account is verified
The blended (frozen) Margarita machine was invented by Dallas restaurateur Mariano Martinez in 1971
Most popular cocktail in the United States by volume; May 5 (Cinco de Mayo) is the highest Margarita sales day
Skinny Margarita omits triple sec in favor of agave nectar; Tommy's Margarita replaces triple sec with agave and no salt
The Cadillac Margarita uses Grand Marnier (a premium Cognac-based orange liqueur) instead of standard triple sec
items.cardBack: Tequila + lime + triple sec (most popular US cocktail)

items.prompt: Which sour cocktail — one of the oldest in the American bartending canon — combines whiskey with lemon juice and simple syrup, and in its 'Boston Sour' variation adds a frothy egg white?
items.explanation:
One of the oldest documented cocktail categories; 'sour' format (spirit + citrus + sweetener) appears in Jerry Thomas's 1862 bartending guide
Standard recipe: 2 oz whiskey (Bourbon or Rye), 3/4 oz fresh lemon juice, 3/4 oz simple syrup; shaken and strained
Boston Sour adds one egg white before shaking ('dry shake' first, then shake with ice) — creates silky foam layer
Served on the rocks or straight up in a coupe; garnished with an orange slice and Luxardo cherry
Rye whiskey was the original base; Bourbon Whiskey Sours are more common today
The sour template applies across spirits: Amaretto Sour, Pisco Sour (Peru's national cocktail), New York Sour adds a red wine float
Pisco Sour (pisco + lime + egg white + Angostura) is Peru's official national cocktail with protected IP
items.cardBack: Whiskey + lemon + sugar (egg white = Boston Sour)

items.prompt: Which high-proof anise-flavored spirit, distilled with wormwood (Artemisia absinthium), fennel, and anise, was nicknamed the 'Green Fairy,' banned in the US in 1912, and re-legalized in 2007 when the 'thujone causes hallucinations' claim was debunked?
items.explanation:
Distilled from green anise, fennel, and wormwood; typically 45–74% ABV — one of the strongest commercially produced spirits
The 'louche' effect: pouring cold water over absinthe turns it milky white as anethole comes out of solution
Traditionally prepared with a slotted spoon and sugar cube over which water is dripped — the absinthe ritual
US ban: 1912 (via the Food and Drugs Act); France and much of Europe followed in 1915; moral panic over thujone (compound in wormwood) allegedly causing hallucinations was scientifically exaggerated
Re-legalized in the US in 2007 by TTB ruling: allowed if thujone content is below 10 ppm
Favored by 19th-century artists and writers: Toulouse-Lautrec, Edgar Degas, Édouard Manet, Arthur Rimbaud, Oscar Wilde, Ernest Hemingway
Modern absinthe contains levels of thujone well below those needed for any psychoactive effect beyond alcohol
